title: Code Review: Add user search with task counts

segments.narration: This PR adds a new user search endpoint for a dashboard widget. The change looks simple: just wiring up a new route.
segments.recommendation: Add authentication middleware before mounting the route: app.use('/api/users', authMiddleware, userRoutes).

segments.narration: Here's the new search endpoint. It accepts a query parameter and validates it. So far, nothing alarming.
segments.recommendation: Add authentication check at the start of the handler: if (!req.user) return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Unauthorized' }).

segments.narration: But wait. The security auditor flagged this immediately: there's no authentication check. Anyone can call this endpoint.
segments.recommendation: Add authentication middleware to verify user session/token before allowing access to user data.

segments.narration: Here's the first critical issue: a full table scan. We fetch ALL users from the database, then filter in JavaScript.
segments.recommendation: Replace full table scan with SQL WHERE clause: SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE name ILIKE $1 OR email ILIKE $1, passing ['%' + q + '%'].

segments.narration: This query has no WHERE clause. With ten thousand users, we're transferring megabytes of data just to search for one name.
segments.recommendation: Use database-side filtering with ILIKE: pool.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name ILIKE $1 OR email ILIKE $1', ['%' + q + '%']).

segments.narration: Now comes the N plus one query anti-pattern. For each matching user, we make a separate database call to count their tasks.
segments.recommendation: Use a single JOIN query: SELECT u.*, COUNT(t.id) as open_task_count FROM users u LEFT JOIN tasks t ON u.id = t.user_id AND t.status = 'open' WHERE (u.name ILIKE $1 OR u.email ILIKE $1) GROUP BY u.id.

segments.narration: If fifty users match the search, that's fifty separate queries. The database connection pool will be exhausted under load.
segments.recommendation: Replace N+1 pattern with single JOIN query to fetch all user task counts in one round-trip.

segments.narration: The junior reviewer asked why we stringify then immediately parse the JSON. There's no reason: it just adds overhead.
segments.recommendation: Remove the unnecessary JSON.stringify/parse cycle. Just return res.json(usersWithTaskCounts) directly.

segments.narration: Finally, the error handling is generic. When this times out at three AM, on-call engineers will have zero debugging information.
segments.recommendation: Add comprehensive logging with error stack traces, query parameters, and timing: console.error('Search failed:', { query: q, error: error.stack }).

segments.narration: The council voted to block this PR. Three critical issues: no authentication, full table scan, and N plus one queries.
segments.recommendation: Fix all three critical issues: add authentication, use SQL WHERE clause with ILIKE, and replace N+1 with JOIN query.
